note: Curated index of Tektronix instructional videos. Reference material — fetch the url for full video content. Transcripts (transcript_chunks[]) are populated by scripts/scrapeTekVideos.ts via Brightcove Playback API + Whisper. Each chunk is {start, end, text} in seconds. Tek videos are hosted on Brightcove with NO published captions, so Whisper is the only transcript path.
updateProcedure: Edit scripts/tek_video_urls.json to add/remove per-family URLs. Then: (1) `npx tsx scripts/scrapeTekVideos.ts --collect` for metadata-only (fast); (2) `npx tsx scripts/scrapeTekVideos.ts --all` for full pipeline including Whisper transcription (slow, needs ffmpeg + openai-whisper in PATH). Sync via V2 → Public → mcp-server.

videos.title: 2 Series MSO Mixed Signal Oscilloscope – A Full Set of Analysis Tools
videos.summary: Overview of the analysis tool set on the 2 Series MSO — measurements, math, cursors, decoding, and related capabilities.

videos.title: 2 Series MSO - Cursors & Measurements
videos.summary: Dedicated tutorial on the 2 Series MSO's cursor and automated measurement workflow — setup, placement, readout interpretation.

videos.title: 2 Series MSO Mixed Signal Oscilloscope Introduction
videos.summary: This video provides a short introduction of the key features and specifications of the 2 Series MSO Mixed Signal Oscilloscope.

videos.title: Making Measurements 2 Series MSO
videos.summary: Walks through three common measurement techniques on the 2 Series MSO: manual on-screen observation, cursor placement, and automated measurements. Start here when teaching someone how to pull numbers off a signal.

videos.title: How to Set Up Vertical Scale
videos.summary:
Getting the vertical scale right is key to accurate, readable measurements. In this quick demo, we walk through how to adjust vertical scale on the Tektronix 2 Series MSO using: 

• The vertical...
videos.youtube_title: How to Set Up Vertical Scale

transcript_chunks.text: The vertical scale controls how much of
your signal's amplitude is visible on
screen. On a digital oscilloscope, the
key is to make the signal as large as
possible without clipping. This uses
more of the available digitizing levels,
reduces quantization errors, and
improves measurement accuracy.
On the 2 series MSO, there are three
simple ways to adjust vertical scale.
One, turn the vertical scale knob, the
fastest and most direct method.
Two, double tap the channel badge, then
type in the exact vertical value you
want.
And three, use touch gestures.
The goal is to fill the screen with your
signal as much as possible, again,
without clipping. If the signal gets
clipped, measurements can become
invalid.
If you're working with multiple signals,
use stacked mode. It gives each channel
its own space, so you don't have to
reduce vertical scale or deal with
overlapping traces. Overlay mode, on the
other hand, puts all signals in the same
window. That often leads to a compromise
in vertical resolution. So for the most
accurate results, maximize your signal
size, avoid clipping, and choose the
display mode that gives you the clarity
you need.

videos.title: 7 Series DPO Highlights
videos.summary: Discover the new Tektronix 7 Series DPO oscilloscope - built for breakthroughs. Custom ASICs and 10-bit ADC deliver exceptional signal fidelity and measurement accuracy.  Analyze faster with...

transcript_chunks.text: In high-speed signal measurements,
jitter and noise distort your data,
leading to unreliable results. Now,
Tektronics most advanced new
oscilloscope brings ultra precision in
both jitter and noise performance,
enabling more margin for your most
difficult testing requirements.
Introducing the 7 series DPO
oscilloscope.
Two new Tektronics custom AS6. The
first, a new ultra low-noise preamp
delivers a high signal to noiseise
ratio, substantially increasing
effective bits and delivering higher
accuracy voltage measurements. The
second, a new 10-bit analog to digital
converter coupled with a new precision
time base reduces jitter to further
enhance signal accuracy.
New high-speed signal path and faster
processing optimizes data flow to speed
up your analysis and testing time. See
signal details on the highdefinition
touchscreen in the blink of an eye and
analyze intuitively with the
award-winning design for touch UI.
And if you need to transfer your data
quickly, the 10 gigabit Ethernet port
combined with the new Tech HSI enables
rapid data transfer so you can get to
your analysis faster. Maximize test
margins with best-in-class noise and
jitter performance with the new 7 Series
DPO. Built for breakthroughs.

What if you could control lab instruments just by telling an AI agent want you want to do?

videos.title: How to Take Measurements on an Oscilloscope
videos.summary: Making measurements on your oscilloscope has never been easier. In this demo, we walk through three common measurement techniques on the Tektronix 2 Series MSO: manual observation, cursor placement...
videos.youtube_title: How to Take Measurements on an Oscilloscope

transcript_chunks.text: Making measurements on the Tektronics 2
series MSO is easier and more intuitive
[music] than ever, especially compared
to previous generations of
oscilloscopes.
While traditional methods like manual
observation and cursor measurements are
still available, the 2 series MSO also
includes a wide range of automatic
measurements [music] along with advanced
features like BOD plots.
Let's begin with a couple of manual
measurement techniques before exploring
the automatic options. You can always
use the vertical and horizontal scales
to estimate [music] basic values like
amplitude and period. For example, using
the probe compensation signal as a test
waveform, [music]
you might notice that a single pulse
spans about one horizontal division. If
your time base is set to 1 millisecond
per division, that means the pulse
period is approximately 1 millisecond.
Another manual method is using [music]
cursors. Place cursor A and cursor B at
the rising edges of two consecutive
pulses. The oscilloscope will display
the delta time, which in this case
[music] should also read around 1
millisecond. These manual methods are
great for quick checks, but they're more
of a rough estimate. For precise [music]
and repeatable results, the built-in
automatic measurements are your best
option. To add an automatic measurement,
simply drag [music] and drop the measure
button onto the channel you want to
analyze. This opens the add measurements
menu [music] where you can choose from a
wide range of options. You can also
press the measure button directly and
manually configure the measurement
parameters. The 2 series MSO includes
over 30 standard automatic measurements
covering both amplitude and timing. And
if you have [music] the two source or
two ultimate options enabled, you'll
also gain access to BOD plots [music]
for frequency response analysis. Whether
you're making quick checks or detailed
analysis, [music] the 2 series MSO helps
you get accurate results quickly [music]
and easily.

videos.title: 7 Series DPO - Maximize Test Margins with Lowest Noise and Highest Effective Numbers of Bits (ENOB)
videos.summary: This video demonstrates how good the noise performance and effective number of bits is on the 7 Series DPO.  You will visually see the difference between the 7 Series DPO with it's 10-bit...
videos.youtube_title: 7 Series DPO - Maximize Test Margins with Lowest Noise and Highest Effective Numbers of Bits (ENOB)

this an ideal signal. This is the gray
ref one waveform at the top of the
display. I'll use this nearly noiseless
signal to compare the performance of two
different oscilloscopes.
I have previously captured the blue ref
sine wave shown on the bottom of the
display as the singleshot acquisition
using a 25 GHz 8bit oscilloscope.
I have previously defined math 2 to be
the ideal minus ref 2 resulting in a
residual or difference waveform from the
8-bit scope indicated by the green math
2 waveform on the bottom of the display.
In a perfectly noiseless system, this
would be a flat line. Now let's acquire
a single shot on channel one of the 7
series DPO.
Performing the same calculation as
before results in a residual or
difference waveform from the 7 series
DPO. Shown here is the orange math 1
waveform in the middle of the display.
It is very easy to see that this results
in a substantially smaller deviation
from the ideal waveform compared to the
8-bit oscilloscope showing the superior
performance of the new front-end design
of the 7 series DPO.
[Music]

transcript_chunks.text: Hello, I'm Sydney Tanaya, an application

model number, serial number, and
firmware revision.

instrument and opened the terminal, I'm
going to mouse over to my system
configurations pane and click on this
tree symbol. This is to fetch my
connected instrument information. Now
I'm going to name my system
configuration MP5000 and it
automatically catches all of the
information including what
instrumentation is currently installed
in each of the MP5000 modular system
slots. And we do this to enable the
language server features such as overhel
which allows me to view all of the
manual information about any TSP command
including usage examples. I can even
expand this into a separate tab which
shows the entire manual page for that
corresponding command. This also enables
command autocomp completion. So as I
start typing out my commands, you can
see that I'm given suggestions for
different autocomp completion options as
well as information about what those
options will do.
To run the script, all I need to do is
right click in my editor and select send
script to terminal. My simple current
sweep is running on my instrument and as
it collects data, it is then printing
those current and voltage values to the
terminal.
If I navigate to the explorer tab, you
could treat this as though it were your

And down here is my script outline. So
it points out any variables I have, all
of my TSP commands, and any other

I'm done with my terminal, all I need to
do is enterit
and both the terminal and the instrument
connection will

videos.title: Chapter 3: MP5000 Series Maximize Automation Control
videos.summary: Integrate the MP5000 seamlessly into your existing automation environment with software frameworks like Python, C# and Labview or with compatible instrument drivers.  Or go farther and enable...
videos.youtube_title: Chapter 3: MP5000 Series Maximize Automation Control

transcript_chunks.text: Integrate the MP5000 seamlessly into
your existing automation environment
with software frameworks like Python, C,
and Lab View, or with compatible
instrument drivers. Go farther and
enable faster test speeds with native
TSP scripting built with TSP toolkit, a
Visual Studio Code extension.
Purpose-built debugging and script
generation tools in TSP toolkit ease the
lift to create your custom test. Export
these test as scripts to combine with
data analysis tools and additional
control code. Need to scale? TSP link
lets you connect up to 32 mainframes and
other TSP enabled instruments into a
unified system with precision
synchronization. Perfect for high
channel count applications.
From faster test execution to reduced
setup time, the MP5000 is built to help
you test more efficiently.
Thanks for watching. And for more
information on the MP5000,
visit tech.com.

summary measurements and plots on two
separate signals in the display,
allowing you to compare the results
directly. You can easily rearrange the
windows to your liking by dragging the
header of a window to a new location and
dropping it where you desire.
Another way to analyze this data is in a
tabular view.
Here
you can see measurement results from the
current acquisition on the left side of
the table as well as statistical
information from multiple acquisitions
across a large population on the right.
This view of measurement data makes
comparing and documenting values easy.
[Music]
With high sample rates and large record
lengths, modern oscilloscopes can
produce multiple gigabytes of waveform
data in just a fraction of a second. In
many applications, getting this data off
the instrument quickly is very
important. While Ethernet has a very
large speed advantage over older
interfaces like GPIB, the overhead of
Skippy commands and the VIX11 protocol
has prevented instruments from being
able to fully realize Ethernet's full
potential. To take advantage of the
speed of Ethernet, we developed
Tektronics high-speed interface or Tech
HSI. Tech HSI is based on gRPC and
protocol buffers to quickly and
efficiently serialize waveform data
produced by the oscilloscope and
transmitted over Ethernet. The 7 series
DPO features a tech HSI server builtin,
allowing client applications to connect
and receive waveform data from the
oscilloscope.
The data is automatically pushed to
connected clients immediately after it
is acquired, ensuring prompt data
transfer with no waveform missed, no
matter how fast the acquisition rate.
Multiple channels worth of data can be
moved all at once, ensuring that all
waveforms are from the same acquisition.
With Tech HSI and the 10 Gbit per second
Ethernet interface on the 7 series DPO,
waveform data can be moved off the scope
at rates of up to 1 gigabyte per second.
To demonstrate this capability, I have
written a Python script that connects to
the TechHI server as a remote client and
simply receives waveform data after each
new acquisition is completed and then
writes it to the disk.
To maximize throughput and keep up with
the stream of incoming waveforms, the
script receives the waveform data on one
thread and writes it to disk using
multiple writer threads
by making use of a Tektronic supplied
library that includes the
electronics.WFM
file format. This script writes the data
to disk in the native tech file format.
This library can be included in your own
Python scripts so that you can save and
recall data inWFM file format or in CSV.
Also included with the tech HSI package,
the TM data types library can convert
WFM files to CSV files or vice versa
with only a few lines of code.
First, I'm going to enable the tech HSI
server on the instrument by navigating
to the utility IO configuration menu.
Inside the high-speed interface panel,
I'll choose SFP+ from the network
connection dropdown so that I can
utilize the port to achieve a much
faster data rate. The SFP plus port is
capable of up to 10 Gbit per second data
rates and can be configured in multiple
ways. Fiber optic SFP plus modules can
be used for longer reaches or when
electrical isolation between the
instrument and the system controller is
required. For many existing
installations, SFP Plus to RJ45 modules
can be used to run 10 Gbit over existing
CAT 5e or higher twisted pair cables.
For shorter reaches under 5 m,
inexpensive direct attach cables can be
used to directly connect to other
network equipment with SFP plus ports.
Finally, I will enable the tech HSI
server by simply turning it on. With a
tech HSI server now enabled, it can
accept connections from remote clients
and will push data to any connected
clients whenever new data is available
without having to query the instrument.
I'm going to run my Python script which
will wait for new waveform data coming
from the 7 series DPO and will simply
save the file to a specific drive
location.
Pressing the single button on the 7
series DPO will acquire an acquisition.
You can see the file quickly show up on
the computer's file browser. Pressing
the single button again shows another
file being saved, indicating no need to
query the oscilloscope for the next
waveform file. The oscilloscope pushed
the data to every connected client via
the tech HSI server. I don't want to
keep pressing single on the
oscilloscope. Instead, I want the flow
of data to be real time as soon as a new
waveform is available. So, I'm going to
press the run button to start continuous
acquisition of waveforms and send each
new waveform to the client PC for
archiving, which this new transfer
protocol can do.
So, let's go ahead and press run.
Wow, look at how quickly this file
browser is filling up with new waveform
files. To show that we have complete
waveforms, I can open one of the
waveform files in Techcope PC, our
offscope version of the oscilloscope
application.
You can see the sine wave from the
instrument here. Take advantage of this
free tech HSI capability along with a 10
GB SFP plus Ethernet port to move data
faster than ever before.
[Music]
The 7 series DPO is designed to grow
with your needs. Buy what you need today
and add capabilities as your
applications evolve.
Some of the key upgrade options
available include optional Windows
operating system, arbitrary function
generator for quickly outputting
standard and custom waveforms, protocol
trigger, decode and search to help
pinpoint complex issues during
debugging. Advanced jitter analysis to
measure timing variations and ensure
high signal integrity and precise
timing. And serial standard compliance
testing packages for validating against
common serial data protocols. Our
engineers continue to add new features
and options regularly. In fact,
enhancements are typically released on a
quarterly basis to help keep your
testing efficient and effective.
With its low noise, high throughput, and
intuitive user interface, this
oscilloscope will be a valuable and
productive tool for years to come.
[Music]

videos.title: Modern Stability Analysis with SEPIA and without Bode Plots
videos.summary:
Steve Sandler, Managing Director of PicoTest, says it is time for a faster and more accurate way to measure modern power systems.
 
In this video, Steve previews SEPIA, Step-Load Enhanced Power...
videos.youtube_title: Modern Stability Analysis with SEPIA and without Bode Plots

transcript_chunks.text: Hi, I'm Steve Sandler, managing director
of PicoTest. Bode plots are dead. I've
been saying that for a long time. I'm
going to tell you why.
>> [music]
>> Today's modern converters are in time
invariant and they're not linear. That's
why we need a better way.
And the better way is using step load
response, which we've always done. I
mean, going back as far as when I was a
young engineer, we did the Bode plot and
then we verified it by doing a step load
response. And I always wondered why that
was. I mean, if we trust the step load,
why do the Bode plot? In 2011, I
developed the math behind Sepia. What
Sepia does is it takes a high-speed load
step response. It takes the result of
that and it transforms that back into
inverse Laplace through a gain function.
From that gain function, we look at the
response. The oscilloscope finds two
peaks in the ringing response and it
displays right in the badge here with
the phase margin. What's the equivalent
circuit model, the LCR network? So, we
walk away with a simulation model we can
use directly in Spice, a quantifiable
phase margin, and it all happens in an
instant. One of the great things about
Sepia is that it doesn't take a lot of
time. Bode plots take maybe 30 seconds
to run.
Running a board like our 2000M board for
30 seconds, you're not doing it without
cooling.
But, a step load response, we can do
that in 500 microseconds. We don't need
any cooling. So, it's simpler, it's
faster, it's more accurate, and it runs
both large signal and small signal for
those non-linear time variant solutions.
Uh for more information, you can contact
us at PicoTest or you can visit
Tektronix and ask them about Sepia.
We're happy to explain to you how it all
works.

transcript_chunks.text: Set up and get testing quickly with the
The main frame features a low profile
touchscreen display for quick
measurement configuration and
diagnostics.
Alternatively, extend control with the
web user interface.
Make basic measurements and view
readings from your PC with a simple user
interface and no code required. Thanks
for watching and for more information on
the MP5000,
visit tech.com.

videos.title: Chapter 1: MP5000 Series Maximize Rack Space Utilization
videos.summary:
Modular. Swappable. Rackable. Watch this video on the groundbreaking form factor of the  new MP5000 Series Modular Precison Test System to see the shape of testing to come.

Learn more about...
videos.youtube_title: Chapter 1: MP5000 Series Maximize Rack Space Utilization

transcript_chunks.text: The MP5000 is a high density SMU and PSU
platform that gives you flexibility and
enables you to maximize rack space
utilization.
Swapping modules to customize your
system is easy. Simply pinch and pull to
release the modules
and push in to replace.
Each module has two channels for up to
six channels in one U mainframe, which
is ideal for high parallel channel count
applications.
The MSMU60-2
is a 60V 1 and 1/2 amp source measure
unit with superior low current accuracy
and a high-speed digitizer for faster
measurements. It's a great solution for
device characterization and measuring
transient behavior. Advanced protection
features ensure your device remains safe
while pushing the limits of its
performance. The MPSU50-2ST
dual 50watt power supply complements the
SMU's versatility with reliable power
channels.
Output up to five amp with a single
channel or stack two channels in
parallel to achieve 10 amp for high
current devices.
Forget swapping leads or adding
protection for your devices. The builtin
bipolar capabilities and output
disconnect relays does it for you.
Thanks for watching and for more
information on the MP5000,
visit tech.com.

videos.title: De-Embedding PCB Cable & Fixture Effects for Accurate Eye Diagram Measurements with an Oscilloscope
videos.summary:
Accurate validation of high-speed designs requires removing signal-path impairments to reveal true performance, particularly for jitter and eye-diagram analysis.

This video demonstrates how...
videos.youtube_title: De-Embedding PCB Cable & Fixture Effects for Accurate Eye Diagram Measurements with an Oscilloscope

transcript_chunks.text: Measurement accuracy can easily be
enhanced by accessing the new SIM or
signal integrity modeling, which allows
you to dembed transmission line effects,
view virtual test points when probing
close to the device under test is not
practical, or simulate extra components
to better reflect the real
effectively performing a simulation of
your design under different conditions.
Here we have an eye diagram of a
high-speed serial signal on channel one
after it has passed through 6.75 in of
printed circuit board trace. We can
clearly see the parasitic effects of the
signal path on the transmitted signal
and how it has caused the eye to close.
We can apply dembedding to remove these
channel effects and view the signal at
the device transmitter even if we can't
access it directly.
Simply tap add new SIM to start building
a new model.
SIM allows you to quickly visualize
changes to the signal path using both a
physical and simulated circuit model.
Physical model represents all physical
components to be de-mbbedded in the
signal path that was used to acquire
waveforms. The simulation model enables
whatif exploration showing how the
waveform would look when an alternate
circuit is embedded. For example, in the
physical model, we can represent the PCB
trace and the 1 meter cable as part of
the measured signal path. But in the
simulation model, these effects are
completely removed and replaced by an
ideal load. SIM introduces simplified
workflows that start with only the
essential blocks and let you add more as
needed, saving time and reducing
complexity by keeping setup focused on
the test at hand. For more demanding
scenarios, the advanced workflow enables
combinations of methods within a single
simulation.
For this scenario, I simply want to
remove the effects of the PCB trace and
the cable. So, I will choose to use the
simple DMBBED workflow.
I will select two port system analysis
since we are using a single cable. If we
were modeling a differential signal
path, we would choose four-port
analysis.
This workflow starts with a single block
in the physical model that is configured
as a through by default. I will use this
block to represent the 1meter cable by
doubletapping to open it for
configuration.
I change the model to S parameters and
ensure the type is set to two port. I
click the browse button to locate and
select the Sparameter file that
represents my cable.
Finally, I rename the block so I can
easily keep track of it in the block
diagram.
Next, I need to model the PCB trace. I
do this by clicking the add block button
and then drag the block into my model,
placing it where it exists in the real
physical system between the cable and
the device transmitter. I will configure
the block and change the model to
Sparameters.
The Sparameters for my PCB trace are
four port. So I will change the type to
four port and then select the file.
In this system I'm only using two ports
but my sparameters are four port. I can
choose which two ports I want to use by
selecting them from the block port
dropdowns eliminating any need to edit
the sparameter files to remap the ports.
I can quickly plot the Sparameters to
verify that my block configuration is
correct. I see the expected transmission
loss profile in the S12 plot. So, I know
that these S parameters and my selected
ports are good.
Finally, I'll rename the block for
tracking purposes.
In the simple DMBBED workflow, the
DMBBED test point has already been added
for us in the simulation model. It is
represented by the green dot that sits
between the device transmitter and the
ideal load. No additional configuration
is needed here. Now that my model is
built, I will tap calculate sim to
compile the model and create the DMBED
test point filter.
Tap okay to go back to the scope
waveform view. We can see a math
waveform representing the waveform at
the DMBBED test point has been added at
the bottom of the display. We can create
an eye height measurement for the new
math 1 waveform and plot an eye diagram.
By dembedding the PCB trace and the 1
meter cable, we can see the open eye at
the device transmitter without any
signal losses.
Comparing the eye height measurements,
we can see that the signal lost over 170
molts of eye height due to impairments
caused by the signal path.
Unlike legacy tools which limit you to
one simulation at a time, now you can
compare practically an unlimited number
of circuit models simultaneously.
Each one outputs to a math waveform and
can be daisy chained for complex what-if
modeling scenarios.
Pairing SIM with jitter analysis enables
comparing multiple eye diagrams and
measurements simultaneously and without
compromise.
Tectronics tools are uniquely designed
to enable simple analysis when
complexity is low and advanced analysis
where multiple sources and measurement
configurations can be analyzed
simultaneously saving significant test
time.
